questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with progressive dyspnea and bilateral lower extremity edema over the past 3 weeks. He reports drinking 8 to 10 beers daily for the past 7 years. On examination, jugular venous distension is noted, and auscultation reveals bibasilar crackles and an S3 heart sound. Chest x-ray shows a cardiothoracic ratio of 0.72. Coronary angiography reveals no significant stenosis. Echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 25% with biventricular dilation and thin ventricular walls. Which of the following best describes the underlying mechanism of cardiac remodeling in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sarcomeres added in parallel along the short axis of myocytes, producing concentric hypertrophy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myocyte disarray with haphazard arrangement of fibers, producing asymmetric hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sarcomeres added in series along the long axis of myocytes, producing eccentric hypertrophy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells, producing restrictive physiology

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fibrofatty replacement of right ventricular myocardium, producing arrhythmogenic remodeling

explanation.summary: This patient with heavy alcohol use has dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM), which produces eccentric hypertrophy through addition of sarcomeres in series.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DCM, increased circulating volume stretches cardiac muscle cells, prompting sarcomeres to be added end-to-end (in series) along the long axis. This produces dilation of cardiac chambers with thin ventricular walls (eccentric hypertrophy), resulting in poor contractile function and systolic dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sarcomeres added in parallel along the short axis produces concentric hypertrophy, which is the pattern seen in pressure-overload conditions such as hypertension or aortic stenosis, not in DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Myocyte disarray with haphazard arrangement of fibers is the histologic hallmark of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), not DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granulomatous inflammation with multinucleated giant cells describes giant cell myocarditis, a rare cause of myocarditis that is not the mechanism of alcohol-related DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fibrofatty replacement of right ventricular myocardium is characteristic of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy, a distinct entity from DCM.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman from rural Bolivia presents with progressive fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and palpitations over the past 6 months. She recalls having a painless swelling around her right eye during childhood that resolved spontaneously. Echocardiography shows biventricular dilation with an ejection fraction of 30%. Endomyocardial biopsy reveals chronic inflammatory infiltrates and fibrosis. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of her cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coxsackie B virus infection causing acute lymphocytic myocarditis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hereditary hemochromatosis causing iron-overload cardiomyopathy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Borrelia burgdorferi infection causing Lyme-associated carditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trypanosoma cruzi infection causing chronic parasitic cardiomyopathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autosomal dominant TTN gene mutation causing familial cardiomyopathy

explanation.summary: The patient from rural Latin America with childhood periorbital swelling (Romana sign) and chronic dilated cardiomyopathy has Chagas disease caused by Trypanosoma cruzi.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chagas disease, caused by Trypanosoma cruzi and transmitted by triatomine (kissing) bugs, is the leading cause of death from parasitic infection in the Western Hemisphere. Unilateral periorbital swelling (Romana sign) is characteristic of the acute stage. Chronic cardiac disease with DCM develops years later and is the most feared complication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coxsackie B is the most common viral cause of myocarditis but typically presents with acute illness rather than a decades-long course, and the geographic and clinical history strongly suggests Chagas disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hereditary hemochromatosis causes iron-overload cardiomyopathy but would not explain the childhood periorbital swelling or the geographic risk factors described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme disease) can cause myocarditis and conduction abnormalities but is not associated with periorbital swelling in childhood or endemic to rural Bolivia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TTN gene mutations cause familial DCM but would not account for the periorbital swelling in childhood or inflammatory infiltrates on biopsy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman who is 2 months postpartum presents with worsening dyspnea and orthopnea over the past week. She had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Physical examination reveals tachycardia, bibasilar crackles, and bilateral pedal edema. Echocardiography shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 28%. Coronary arteries are normal. In addition to standard heart failure therapy with diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and beta-blockers, which of the following is most specifically indicated for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate cardiac transplantation evaluation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anticoagulation therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous immunoglobulin therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alcohol septal ablation of the interventricular septum

choices.label: E
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroid pulse therapy

explanation.summary: This patient has peripartum cardiomyopathy, which in addition to standard heart failure therapy specifically requires anticoagulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is a rare form of DCM seen in late pregnancy or up to 5 months postpartum. In patients presenting with peripartum cardiomyopathy, anticoagulants are specifically indicated in addition to standard heart failure therapy (diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and beta-blockers) due to the high thrombotic risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cardiac transplantation is reserved for refractory patients who do not respond to medical therapy; it is not the immediate next step in a newly diagnosed peripartum cardiomyopathy case.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intravenous immunoglobulin is not part of the standard management of peripartum cardiomyopathy and lacks evidence for routine use in this condition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Alcohol septal ablation is a procedure used in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with severe outflow tract obstruction, not in dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-dose corticosteroids are not indicated in peripartum cardiomyopathy and are reserved for specific inflammatory conditions like giant cell myocarditis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use for 12 years presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and lower extremity swelling. He also has a history of liver cirrhosis. Echocardiography demonstrates biventricular dilation with an ejection fraction of 22%. He is started on appropriate heart failure medications. At a follow-up visit 6 months later, after complete alcohol cessation, repeat echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 42% with reduced chamber dimensions. Which of the following best explains the improvement observed in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reversal of direct myocardial toxic injury following removal of the offending agent

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spontaneous regression of an underlying autoimmune inflammatory process

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resolution of iron overload from concurrent hereditary hemochromatosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compensatory concentric hypertrophy replacing the previously dilated chambers

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of collateral coronary circulation restoring myocardial perfusion

explanation.summary: Alcohol-induced DCM can be partially or fully reversible with complete alcohol cessation because alcohol is directly toxic to myocardial cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alcohol is directly toxic to myocardial cells. Alcohol toxicity of the heart can sometimes reverse with alcohol cessation, as the removal of the ongoing toxic insult allows myocardial recovery and improvement in left ventricular function.

whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no autoimmune inflammatory process in alcoholic cardiomyopathy; the damage is directly toxic rather than immune-mediated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Although iron overload can cause DCM, this patient's presentation is consistent with alcoholic cardiomyopathy, and the improvement correlates with alcohol cessation rather than iron removal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Concentric hypertrophy is a response to pressure overload, not the mechanism of recovery in DCM; recovery involves normalization of chamber dimensions and improved contractility.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Collateral coronary circulation develops in ischemic heart disease, not in toxic cardiomyopathy; this patient has no evidence of coronary artery disease.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man presents with 5 days of fever, malaise, and progressive dyspnea. He recently recovered from an upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago. Examination reveals tachycardia, an S3 gallop, and fine bibasilar crackles. Echocardiography shows global hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of 35% and biventricular dilation. An endomyocardial biopsy is performed. Which of the following histological findings would most likely be seen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Neutrophilic infiltrates with abscess formation and myocyte necrosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltrates with peripheral blood eosinophilia and rash

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocyte disarray with whorled pattern of haphazardly arranged fibers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Noncaseating granulomas with multinucleated giant cells and fibrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphocytic infiltrates with evidence of myocardial necrosis

explanation.summary: Post-viral myocarditis causing DCM characteristically shows lymphocytic infiltrates with myocardial necrosis on biopsy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Viral myocarditis is the most common cause of infectious myocarditis and characteristically presents with lymphocytic infiltrates and myocyte necrosis on histology. This patient's recent upper respiratory infection followed by heart failure symptoms is a classic presentation of viral myocarditis leading to DCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Neutrophilic infiltrates with abscess formation are characteristic of bacterial myocarditis, which is less common and typically occurs in the setting of bacteremia rather than following an upper respiratory infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Eosinophilic infiltrates with eosinophilia and rash suggest hypersensitivity myocarditis, often caused by drug reactions (e.g., dopamine, diuretics), not viral infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocyte disarray with a whorled pattern is pathognomonic for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, not viral myocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Noncaseating granulomas suggest sarcoidosis or giant cell myocarditis, not viral myocarditis following an upper respiratory infection.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer is being treated with combination chemotherapy. She presents with new-onset exertional dyspnea and pedal edema. Echocardiography reveals a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 30%, diffusely thin ventricular walls, and no valvular abnormalities. Her oncologist is concerned about drug-induced cardiomyopathy. Which of the following chemotherapy agents is most likely responsible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cyclophosphamide

choices.label: B
choices.text: Doxorubicin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Methotrexate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cisplatin

choices.label: E
choices.text: 5-Fluorouracil

explanation.summary: Doxorubicin is the chemotherapy drug most classically associated with dose-dependent dilated cardiomyopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Doxorubicin is a well-known cause of drug-induced DCM. It is directly toxic to myocardial cells, although the exact mechanism is not well understood. The source specifically lists doxorubicin as a key drug cause of DCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cyclophosphamide can cause cardiotoxicity but it is not the chemotherapy drug most classically associated with dilated cardiomyopathy as described in the source material.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methotrexate is not typically associated with dilated cardiomyopathy; its major toxicities involve the liver, lungs, and bone marrow.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cisplatin is associated with nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity but is not a classic cause of dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 5-Fluorouracil can cause coronary vasospasm and ischemia but is not a classic cause of dilated cardiomyopathy with chamber dilation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with recently diagnosed dilated cardiomyopathy of unknown cause is being evaluated. His father and paternal uncle both died of heart failure before age 50. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the TTN gene. Which of the following best describes the inheritance pattern most commonly associated with familial dilated cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: X-linked recessive with variable penetrance in carrier females

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autosomal recessive requiring homozygosity for clinical expression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mitochondrial inheritance passed exclusively through the maternal line

choices.label: D
choices.text: Polygenic inheritance with no identifiable single-gene contribution

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autosomal dominant with mutations in sarcomere protein genes

explanation.summary: Familial DCM most commonly follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, often involving mutations in sarcomere genes such as TTN.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most rare inherited forms of DCM are autosomal dominant. More than 30 mutations are associated with DCM, often in sarcomere proteins. The TTN gene, which encodes the titin protein in the sarcomere, is one such commonly mutated gene. The resultant sarcomere dysfunction causes the systolic dysfunction characteristic of DCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While X-linked inheritance has been observed in some forms of DCM, it is not the most common inheritance pattern; autosomal dominant is most common.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autosomal recessive inheritance has been observed in rare cases of DCM, but autosomal dominant is the predominant pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitochondrial inheritance can cause cardiomyopathies in rare cases, but it is not the most common inheritance pattern for familial DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Familial DCM has identifiable single-gene mutations (e.g., TTN), and the most common pattern is autosomal dominant, not polygenic.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman is evaluated for progressive heart failure. She has a history of chronic alcohol use but stopped drinking 3 years ago. Echocardiography confirms DCM with an ejection fraction of 20%. Despite optimal medical therapy, her symptoms continue to worsen over the next year. She develops recurrent hospitalizations for decompensated heart failure. She is referred for advanced therapies. Which of the following is the most common cause of death in patients with dilated cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Progressive renal failure secondary to chronic low cardiac output

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acute aortic dissection due to dilated cardiac chambers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sudden arrhythmia or embolic event

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rupture of the thinned ventricular free wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infectious endocarditis from turbulent intracardiac blood flow

explanation.summary: Most patients with DCM die as the result of sudden onset arrhythmia or from an embolic event.
explanation.whyCorrect: In DCM, after diagnosis about 80% of patients live through the first year, with about 10% dying each subsequent year. Most patients die as the result of a sudden onset arrhythmia or from an embolic event.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While cardiorenal syndrome can develop in advanced heart failure, progressive renal failure is not cited as the most common cause of death in DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic dissection is not associated with DCM; it is related to conditions such as hypertension, Marfan syndrome, or connective tissue disorders.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ventricular free wall rupture is a complication of acute myocardial infarction, not a typical cause of death in dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Infectious endocarditis is not a recognized common cause of death in DCM patients; it is more associated with valvular abnormalities or intravenous drug use.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with fatigue and exertional dyspnea that has been worsening over the past 2 months. He takes no medications and denies alcohol or drug use. Family history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals an S3 gallop and bilateral pedal edema. Echocardiography shows biventricular dilation with an ejection fraction of 28%. Coronary angiography is normal. Endomyocardial biopsy shows myocyte hypertrophy, cytoplasmic vacuolizations, and interstitial fibrosis without inflammatory infiltrates. Serum BNP is markedly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viral myocarditis causing acute dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eosinophilic myocarditis from hypersensitivity drug reaction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Restrictive cardiomyopathy from cardiac amyloidosis

explanation.summary: The absence of inflammatory infiltrates, toxic exposures, or identifiable causes combined with nonspecific histological findings (myocyte hypertrophy, vacuolizations, fibrosis) points to idiopathic DCM.
explanation.whyCorrect: Idiopathic DCM refers to cases where the cause is unknown. Histological findings in noninflammatory causes of DCM are often nonspecific and include myocyte hypertrophy, cytoplasmic vacuolizations, and interstitial fibrosis. The elevated BNP reflects the dilated ventricles. With no identifiable cause (no alcohol, drugs, infection, or family history), this is classified as idiopathic DCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Viral myocarditis would show lymphocytic infiltrates with myocardial necrosis on biopsy, not the noninflammatory pattern described here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents with thickened ventricular walls and preserved or small cavity size, not biventricular dilation and thin walls with low ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eosinophilic myocarditis would show predominant eosinophilic infiltrates on biopsy, often with peripheral eosinophilia and rash, which are absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Restrictive cardiomyopathy from amyloidosis would show amyloid deposits on biopsy and present with diastolic dysfunction, not biventricular dilation with systolic failure.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with a history of chronic alcohol use disorder is admitted with decompensated heart failure. He is malnourished and reports eating very little over the past several months. Physical examination reveals tachycardia, warm extremities, significant bilateral lower extremity edema, and hepatomegaly. Echocardiography shows a dilated left ventricle with global hypokinesis. Laboratory studies reveal low serum albumin. The physician suspects that nutritional deficiency may be contributing to his cardiac dysfunction. Supplementation of which of the following would most directly address this nutritional contributor to his cardiomyopathy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thiamine supplementation to treat wet beriberi-associated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Niacin supplementation to reverse pellagra-associated cardiac dysfunction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Folate supplementation to correct megaloblastic anemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin C supplementation to address scurvy-related myocardial weakness

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron supplementation to correct microcytic anemia impairing oxygen delivery

explanation.summary: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency causes wet beriberi, which is associated with dilated cardiomyopathy, particularly in malnourished patients with chronic alcohol use.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thiamine deficiency, also known as wet beriberi when associated with DCM, can cause cardiac damage and lead to DCM. It is uncommon in developed countries except in malnourished people, such as those with chronic alcohol use disorder who often have poor nutritional intake.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Niacin deficiency causes pellagra (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia) but is not specifically associated with dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia but does not directly cause dilated cardiomyopathy; it is not the nutritional cause of wet beriberi.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy with bleeding gums and poor wound healing, but it is not a recognized cause of dilated cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron deficiency causes microcytic anemia and high-output cardiac states, but iron supplementation does not address thiamine-deficiency cardiomyopathy; notably, iron overload (not deficiency) is a separate cause of DCM.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of chest pain, fever, and fatigue. He was recently started on a new medication by his psychiatrist. Physical examination reveals a diffuse erythematous rash, tachycardia, and bibasilar crackles. Laboratory studies show peripheral blood eosinophilia. Echocardiography reveals a mildly dilated left ventricle with reduced systolic function. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for this patient's presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lithium carbonate for bipolar disorder management

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sertraline for major depressive disorder management

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bupropion for smoking cessation therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Buspirone for generalized anxiety disorder management

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amitriptyline for major depressive disorder management

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline can cause eosinophilic (hypersensitivity) myocarditis and dilated cardiomyopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tricyclic antidepressants are listed as drugs that can lead to DCM. Eosinophilic myocarditis is caused by hypersensitivity reactions to medications. The triad of rash, fever, and peripheral eosinophilia alongside heart failure symptoms in a patient recently started on a tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline) is consistent with drug-induced hypersensitivity myocarditis leading to DCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lithium can have cardiac effects but is not specifically listed among the drugs that commonly cause DCM or hypersensitivity myocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sertraline is an SSRI and is not among the drug classes commonly associated with DCM or eosinophilic myocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bupropion is an atypical antidepressant and is not listed among the medications commonly causing DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Buspirone is an anxiolytic that is not associated with myocarditis or dilated cardiomyopathy.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with newly diagnosed dilated cardiomyopathy of unclear etiology undergoes extensive workup. His cardiologist is deciding whether to proceed with endomyocardial biopsy. The patient has no history of alcohol use, drug exposure, recent infection, or family history of cardiac disease. His echocardiogram shows biventricular dilation with an ejection fraction of 22%. Which of the following clinical scenarios would most strongly warrant proceeding with endomyocardial biopsy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Well-established chronic alcoholic cardiomyopathy with known etiology

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clearly documented familial cardiomyopathy with known genetic mutation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Longstanding hypertensive heart disease with gradual systolic decline

choices.label: D
choices.text: Previously confirmed viral myocarditis with stable cardiac function

choices.label: E
choices.text: Unexplained cause of new-onset heart failure requiring tissue diagnosis

explanation.summary: Myocardial biopsy is most useful and commonly performed in cases of unexplained new-onset heart failure where tissue diagnosis may guide management.
explanation.whyCorrect: While DCM can often be diagnosed without biopsy based on clinical exam and radiographic findings (especially when there is an obvious underlying cause), myocardial biopsy is more useful and commonly performed in cases with an unexplained cause of new-onset heart failure, exactly as in this patient with no identifiable etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In patients with an obvious underlying cause like heavy alcohol use, the diagnosis of DCM can be established without biopsy based on clinical and imaging findings.
whyWrongByChoice.B: When familial cardiomyopathy is already confirmed with a known genetic mutation, biopsy adds little diagnostic value.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypertensive heart disease with systolic decline has a clear etiology and does not require tissue biopsy for diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Previously confirmed viral myocarditis with stable function does not require repeat biopsy, as the diagnosis has already been established.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a 20-year history of poorly controlled hypertension presents with exertional dyspnea that has progressively worsened over 6 months. His blood pressure is 172/98 mm Hg. Echocardiography reveals a left ventricular wall thickness of 16 mm with a normal-sized left ventricular cavity and an ejection fraction of 60%. Doppler studies show impaired diastolic filling. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which the left ventricle has adapted to this patient's chronic condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sarcomeres added in series along the longitudinal axis, producing eccentric hypertrophy with chamber dilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sarcomeres added in parallel along the short axis, producing concentric hypertrophy with decreased compliance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocyte disarray with fibrocollagenous tissue deposition, producing asymmetric septal thickening

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrofatty infiltration of the ventricular wall, producing thinning and arrhythmogenic substrate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Amyloid deposition between myocytes, producing restrictive filling and biventricular wall thickening

explanation.summary: Chronic hypertension causes pressure overload, leading to concentric hypertrophy through parallel addition of sarcomeres, resulting in a thick, stiff, noncompliant ventricular wall.
explanation.whyCorrect: In concentric hypertrophy from chronic hypertension, the persistently elevated LV pressure causes sarcomeres to be added parallel to one another. This leads to increased wall thickness with normal or decreased cavity size, producing a thick, stiff, noncompliant ventricular wall that does not stretch well enough to fill during diastole (diastolic dysfunction).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Sarcomeres added in series produce eccentric hypertrophy with chamber dilation, as seen in dilated cardiomyopathy from volume overload, not the pressure overload of hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocyte disarray with fibrocollagenous deposition is the histological hallmark of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), a genetic disorder, not hypertensive heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrofatty infiltration of the ventricular wall is characteristic of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy, not hypertensive LVH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Amyloid deposition describes cardiac amyloidosis, a form of restrictive cardiomyopathy, not hypertensive concentric LVH.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old male college basketball player collapses during practice and is found to be in ventricular fibrillation. He is successfully resuscitated. His father died suddenly at age 35 during exercise. Echocardiography reveals asymmetric septal hypertrophy with a septal thickness of 22 mm and systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the gene encoding the beta-myosin heavy chain. Which of the following inheritance patterns is most consistent with this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autosomal dominant with variable genetic penetrance across family members

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autosomal recessive with complete penetrance in homozygous individuals

choices.label: C
choices.text: X-linked dominant affecting males and females equally in all generations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitochondrial inheritance with maternal transmission and heteroplasmy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autosomal codominant requiring both alleles for phenotypic expression

explanation.summary: HCM is caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere genes with variable genetic penetrance.
explanation.whyCorrect: 60-70% of HCM cases are caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere genes, with the beta-myosin heavy chain being one of the most commonly mutated genes. There is variable genetic penetrance, so the family hereditary pattern may not appear classic for autosomal dominant disorders.

whyWrongByChoice.B: HCM follows autosomal dominant, not autosomal recessive, inheritance and does not require homozygosity for expression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HCM is not X-linked; it is caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere protein genes located on autosomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitochondrial inheritance is not the mechanism for HCM; the causative mutations are in nuclear genes encoding sarcomere proteins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Codominant inheritance is not the pattern for HCM; a single mutant allele is sufficient for disease expression, consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with known hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents for routine follow-up. She reports mild exertional dyspnea but no syncope. On auscultation, a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur is heard at the left sternal border. To differentiate this murmur from aortic stenosis, you ask the patient to perform the Valsalva maneuver. Which of the following best describes the expected change in her murmur and the underlying mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Murmur decreases because increased venous return dilates the outflow tract and relieves obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Murmur remains unchanged because the Valsalva maneuver does not alter dynamic outflow obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Murmur increases because decreased venous return worsens anterior motion of the mitral valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Murmur decreases because decreased venous return reduces turbulent flow across the stenotic valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: Murmur increases because increased intrathoracic pressure directly compresses the hypertrophied septum

explanation.summary: The Valsalva maneuver decreases venous return, which in HCM worsens the outflow obstruction by allowing greater systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, increasing the murmur.
explanation.whyCorrect: The Valsalva maneuver reduces venous return to the heart. In HCM, decreased venous return increases the intensity of the murmur because outflow obstruction is worsened by greater anterior motion of the mitral valve. This is opposite to aortic stenosis, where decreased blood flow through the stenotic area decreases the murmur.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Valsalva maneuver decreases, not increases, venous return, so there is no dilation of the outflow tract; the murmur increases, not decreases.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The Valsalva maneuver does alter dynamic outflow obstruction in HCM by reducing venous return and worsening systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: This describes the response of an aortic stenosis murmur to Valsalva, not HCM. In aortic stenosis, less blood over the stenotic area decreases the murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The mechanism is not direct compression of the septum by intrathoracic pressure; rather, it is decreased venous return leading to worsened mitral valve anterior motion and outflow obstruction.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a 15-year history of hypertension presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation and exertional dyspnea. Her blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg on current medications. Echocardiography shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with impaired diastolic relaxation and preserved ejection fraction. Her physician wants to initiate antihypertensive therapy aimed at reducing her LVH. Which of the following medications should be avoided because it may worsen left ventricular outflow obstruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lisinopril, an angiotensin-converting-enzyme inhibitor

choices.label: B
choices.text: Losartan, an angiotensin receptor blocker

choices.label: C
choices.text: Amlodipine, a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digoxin, a cardiac glycoside

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hydrochlorothiazide, a thiazide diuretic

explanation.summary: In patients with LVH, digoxin should be avoided as it can worsen outflow obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source states that for LVH secondary to hypertension, the recommended antihypertensive drugs are ACE inhibitors, ARBs, CCBs, or thiazide diuretics. Nitrates, beta-blockers, and digoxin should be avoided as they worsen the outflow obstruction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE inhibitors like lisinopril are among the recommended antihypertensive drugs for LVH secondary to hypertension and can cause regression of LVH.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ARBs like losartan are among the recommended antihypertensive drugs for treating hypertension-related LVH.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Calcium channel blockers like amlodipine are recommended antihypertensive agents for LVH management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thiazide diuretics like hydrochlorothiazide are among the recommended drugs for LVH secondary to hypertension.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and documented left ventricular outflow tract obstruction presents with progressive exertional dyspnea despite maximal medical therapy with metoprolol and verapamil. Echocardiography shows a resting outflow tract gradient of 65 mm Hg with severe asymmetric septal hypertrophy. His cardiologist recommends an interventional procedure. Which of the following procedures involves intentionally inducing a controlled myocardial infarction to reduce the obstruction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Percutaneous coronary intervention with drug-eluting stent placement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Alcohol septal ablation with ethanol injection into a septal perforator artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical septal myomectomy with excision of hypertrophied muscle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral valve replacement to eliminate systolic anterior motion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement for arrhythmia prevention

explanation.summary: Alcohol septal ablation involves injecting ethanol into a septal perforator artery to cause a controlled myocardial infarction of the hypertrophied septum, reducing outflow obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: For HCM patients with extreme septal hypertrophy and outflow tract obstruction refractory to medical therapy, alcohol septal ablation (causing a myocardial infarction of the septal muscle) can drastically improve symptoms by reducing the septal mass that obstructs the outflow tract.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Percutaneous coronary intervention with stenting treats coronary artery disease, not hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; it does not reduce septal hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgical septal myomectomy involves cutting out the extra muscle, not inducing a myocardial infarction; while it is an alternative procedure for HCM, it does not match the description of intentionally inducing infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral valve replacement is not the standard approach for HCM with outflow obstruction; the primary interventions target the hypertrophied septum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator prevents sudden cardiac death from arrhythmias but does not reduce outflow tract obstruction or address the hemodynamic problem.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old competitive swimmer is found to have left ventricular hypertrophy on a routine pre-participation ECG. He is asymptomatic and has no family history of cardiac disease. His blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg. An echocardiogram is ordered to further evaluate. Which of the following echocardiographic findings would most strongly suggest hypertrophic cardiomyopathy rather than physiologic athlete's heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Symmetric mild increase in LV wall thickness with proportionally enlarged chamber

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal diastolic function with increased stroke volume and cardiac output

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mildly dilated left ventricle with preserved ejection fraction and no valve abnormalities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asymmetric interventricular septal thickening with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: Normal LV wall thickness with mildly increased right ventricular dimensions

explanation.summary: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve is characteristic of HCM and distinguishes it from the symmetric, proportional hypertrophy of athlete's heart.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hypertrophy in HCM is typically asymmetric, with dominant thickening of the interventricular septum. Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve is a characteristic finding in HCM that causes outflow obstruction. These findings clearly distinguish HCM from athlete's heart, which shows symmetric and proportional hypertrophy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Symmetric mild wall thickening with proportionally enlarged chamber is more consistent with physiologic athlete's heart than HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Normal diastolic function with increased stroke volume is characteristic of well-conditioned athlete's heart, not pathologic HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mildly dilated LV with preserved ejection fraction and no valve abnormalities is more consistent with athlete's heart or early dilated cardiomyopathy, not HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Normal LV wall thickness with increased RV dimensions does not suggest HCM, which is characterized by increased LV (especially septal) wall thickness.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of chronic hypertension and LVH presents with chest pain during physical exertion. Coronary angiography reveals no significant coronary artery stenosis. Her troponin is mildly elevated. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of her ischemia in the absence of coronary artery disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary vasospasm triggered by endothelial dysfunction from chronic hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thrombotic occlusion of a small coronary branch from a mural thrombus in the left atrium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased myocardial oxygen demand from hypertrophied tissue and compression of intramural microvasculature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic root dilation causing ostial compression of the left main coronary artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pericardial effusion causing external compression of the epicardial coronary arteries

explanation.summary: In LVH, chest pain from ischemia results from increased oxygen demand due to greater tissue mass and compression/distortion of the microvasculature reducing oxygen delivery.
explanation.whyCorrect: The extra tissue volume associated with LVH increases oxygen demand and causes tissue ischemia. Additionally, the increased tissue volume compresses and distorts the microvasculature, further diminishing oxygen delivery. Together these factors exacerbate the unmet oxygen demand, producing chest pain even without epicardial coronary artery disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While coronary vasospasm can cause chest pain, the source specifically attributes ischemia in LVH to increased oxygen demand and microvasculature compression, not vasospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mural thrombi can occur in LVH patients, but this mechanism would typically cause a focal infarct from embolic occlusion, not the demand-supply mismatch ischemia described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic root dilation with ostial compression is not a recognized mechanism of ischemia in LVH; it is more relevant to conditions like aortitis or aortic aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pericardial effusion compressing epicardial arteries is not the mechanism of ischemia in LVH; pericardial effusion causes tamponade physiology, not supply-demand mismatch.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man with known hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is seen in clinic for exertional dyspnea and occasional presyncope during exercise. He has a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur that increases when he stands up from a supine position. His echocardiogram shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy and a resting LVOT gradient of 40 mm Hg. Which of the following medications is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Furosemide to reduce intravascular volume and decrease cardiac preload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lisinopril to reduce afterload and promote regression of hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isosorbide mononitrate to cause venodilation and reduce preload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Digoxin to improve contractility and increase cardiac output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Metoprolol to reduce heart rate and decrease myocardial oxygen demand

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers like metoprolol are first-line treatment for symptomatic HCM, as they reduce heart rate, decrease oxygen demand, and allow more time for diastolic filling.
explanation.whyCorrect: For symptomatic HCM patients with dyspnea or chest pain, the best drugs are beta-blockers or verapamil. Metoprolol (a beta-blocker) is appropriate first-line therapy as it reduces heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand while allowing more diastolic filling time.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diuretics like furosemide should be used cautiously in HCM and only if there is severe volume overload, because dehydrating the patient will potentially worsen the outflow obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE inhibitors like lisinopril are recommended for LVH secondary to hypertension, but they are not first-line for HCM, where the goal is to reduce obstruction rather than afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitrates like isosorbide mononitrate should be avoided in patients with LVH as they worsen outflow obstruction by reducing preload and ventricular volume.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Digoxin should be avoided in patients with LVH/HCM as it can worsen outflow tract obstruction.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a myocardial biopsy specimen from a 28-year-old man who died suddenly during a marathon. The specimen shows markedly hypertrophied myocytes arranged in a whorled, disorganized pattern with myocytes oriented perpendicular and oblique to each other. There is extensive intervening fibrocollagenous tissue between the myocytes. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with these histological findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with myocyte disarray and interstitial fibrosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy with eccentric hypertrophy and vacuolated myocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viral myocarditis with lymphocytic infiltrates and myocyte necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Restrictive cardiomyopathy with amyloid deposition between myocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy with fibrofatty replacement

explanation.summary: Myocyte disarray with a whorled pattern of haphazardly arranged myocytes and interstitial fibrocollagenous tissue is the histological hallmark of HCM.
explanation.whyCorrect: The morphologic changes in HCM characteristically comprise myocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, and myocyte disarray. Myocyte disarray refers to a whorled pattern of haphazardly arranged myocytes, arranged not parallel but perpendicular and oblique to each other, with excessive and abnormal fibrocollagenous tissue deposits between the myocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: DCM shows myocyte hypertrophy with cytoplasmic vacuolizations and interstitial fibrosis, but myocytes are lengthened rather than disarrayed in a whorled pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Viral myocarditis shows lymphocytic infiltrates with myocyte necrosis, not the whorled disarray pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Restrictive cardiomyopathy from amyloidosis shows amorphous eosinophilic deposits (amyloid) between myocytes, not the characteristic myocyte disarray of HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy shows replacement of RV myocardium with fibrofatty tissue, not the whorled myocyte disarray described.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with a 25-year history of hypertension has an ECG performed during a routine visit. The ECG shows tall R waves in leads V5-V6 and deep S waves in lead V1. The sum of the S wave amplitude in V1 and the R wave amplitude in V5 is 48 mm. Inverted T waves are noted in the lateral leads. Which of the following is the most likely interpretation of these ECG findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right ventricular hypertrophy with right axis deviation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anterior wall myocardial infarction with ST elevation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left bundle branch block with secondary repolarization changes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with delta wave formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular hypertrophy with repolarization abnormalities

explanation.summary: High voltage in lateral leads (V5-V6) with a sum of S in V1 plus R in V5/V6 exceeding 35 mm, along with T-wave inversions, is the classic ECG pattern of LVH.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most patients with LVH show ECG changes of high voltage in the lateral leads (I, V5-V6). A common criterion for LVH is adding the S amplitude in V1 to the R amplitude in V5 or V6; if more than 35 mm, LVH is present. At 48 mm, this clearly meets criteria. The inverted T waves in lateral leads are typical repolarization abnormalities seen with LVH.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right ventricular hypertrophy shows tall R waves in right precordial leads (V1-V2) and right axis deviation, not the left-sided voltage pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Anterior wall MI shows ST elevation or pathologic Q waves in anterior leads, not the high-voltage pattern with T-wave inversions in lateral leads.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Left bundle branch block shows wide QRS complexes with characteristic morphology, not simply high-voltage R and S waves meeting LVH voltage criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome shows a short PR interval and delta waves, which are not described in this ECG.

questions.stem: A 21-year-old woman with known hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presents to her cardiologist with worsening exertional dyspnea. On auscultation, a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur is heard. When the patient squats, the murmur intensity decreases. Which of the following best explains why squatting diminishes the murmur in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Squatting decreases systemic vascular resistance, reducing the pressure gradient across the aortic valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Squatting increases venous return, which better fills the ventricle and relieves outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Squatting compresses the abdominal aorta, reducing afterload and decreasing turbulent flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Squatting increases intrathoracic pressure, reducing left ventricular preload and contractility

choices.label: E
choices.text: Squatting activates the parasympathetic nervous system, slowing heart rate and reducing obstruction

explanation.summary: Squatting increases venous return to the heart, which better fills the ventricle and approximates the mitral valve, relieving some of the dynamic outflow obstruction in HCM.
explanation.whyCorrect: Contraction of the leg muscles during squatting returns more blood to the heart. In HCM, the extra blood better approximates the mitral valve, actually relieving some of the obstruction. This in effect decreases the murmur intensity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squatting actually increases systemic vascular resistance by compressing the femoral arteries; it does not decrease SVR, and the murmur in HCM is from dynamic outflow obstruction, not a fixed aortic valve stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While squatting may increase afterload, the decrease in HCM murmur is due to increased venous return filling the ventricle and relieving obstruction, not aortic compression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Squatting does not significantly increase intrathoracic pressure like the Valsalva maneuver; it increases venous return, which is the opposite effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The primary mechanism is hemodynamic (increased venous return), not a parasympathetic reflex reducing heart rate.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis presents with progressive dyspnea and chest pain on exertion. Echocardiography shows a heavily calcified aortic valve with a peak gradient of 70 mm Hg and an aortic valve area of 0.7 cm squared. The left ventricular wall thickness is 15 mm with a normal cavity size and preserved ejection fraction. Which of the following types of heart failure is this patient most likely to develop as her condition progresses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systolic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction from sarcomere loss

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-output heart failure from increased metabolic demand of hypertrophied muscle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diastolic heart failure with reduced end-diastolic volume from impaired ventricular filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right-sided heart failure from pulmonary hypertension caused by mitral valve prolapse

choices.label: E
choices.text: Obstructive heart failure from complete occlusion of the aortic valve orifice

explanation.summary: Aortic stenosis causes LVH through concentric hypertrophy, leading to a stiff, noncompliant ventricle with impaired diastolic filling and diastolic heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: As the ventricular chamber size decreases due to wall thickening from concentric hypertrophy, the smaller LV cavity leads to reduced end-diastolic volume and resultant stroke volume, leading to decreased cardiac output. This is diastolic dysfunction, where the reduction in output stems from ineffective filling during diastole, not from ineffective contraction during systole.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Systolic heart failure with reduced EF is characteristic of dilated cardiomyopathy, not the concentric hypertrophy pattern from aortic stenosis, where the primary dysfunction is diastolic.
whyWrongByChoice.B: High-output heart failure occurs in conditions like anemia, thyrotoxicosis, or arteriovenous fistulas, not in the setting of pressure-overload LVH from aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While pulmonary hypertension can eventually develop from longstanding left heart failure, mitral valve prolapse is not the mechanism in aortic stenosis-induced LVH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Complete occlusion of the aortic valve orifice is not how aortic stenosis progresses; the valve remains patent but severely stenotic, and heart failure results from the myocardial consequences of chronic pressure overload.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and documented left ventricular outflow tract obstruction is evaluated for worsening symptoms despite beta-blocker therapy. Echocardiography shows systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with a posteriorly directed regurgitant jet. Which of the following best explains why mitral regurgitation develops in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rheumatic inflammation causing fibrosis and retraction of the mitral valve leaflets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve leaflets with prolapse into the left atrium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Papillary muscle rupture from acute ischemia causing flail mitral valve leaflet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Abnormal anterior motion of the mitral valve preventing appropriate valve closure during systole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dilation of the mitral valve annulus from progressive left ventricular chamber enlargement

explanation.summary: In HCM, systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve impairs valve closure, causing blood to pass backward through the incompletely closed valve during systole, resulting in mitral regurgitation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hypertrophy in HCM can distort the mitral valve. During systole, the mitral valve moves anteriorly (SAM), impinging on the outflow tract. Because the valve is not appropriately closed during systole, blood also passes backward through the valve, leading to mitral regurgitation, which can lead to left atrial dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rheumatic valve disease causes fibrosis and commissural fusion, typically leading to mitral stenosis; it is not the mechanism in HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Myxomatous degeneration causes mitral valve prolapse, a distinct condition unrelated to the SAM mechanism in HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Papillary muscle rupture occurs as a complication of acute myocardial infarction, not as a feature of HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Annular dilation from chamber enlargement causes functional mitral regurgitation in dilated cardiomyopathy, not in HCM where the mechanism is SAM-related.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with recently diagnosed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy asks about his prognosis. He has mild symptoms controlled with verapamil and has been advised to avoid competitive sports. His family history is notable for a brother who had an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placed at age 38. The patient asks about the overall annual mortality rate for patients with HCM. Which of the following is the most accurate response regarding HCM-related mortality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overall mortality is similar to the general population at approximately 1%, but individual risk varies substantially

choices.label: B
choices.text: Annual mortality is approximately 10%, with most deaths due to progressive systolic heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Annual mortality is approximately 5%, primarily from thromboembolic stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Annual mortality exceeds 15% in all patients regardless of risk stratification

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mortality is negligible with current therapies, and life expectancy is normal for all patients

explanation.summary: The overall mortality in HCM is similar to that of the general population (approximately 1%), although prognosis varies substantially and requires individualized risk stratification.
explanation.whyCorrect: The overall mortality in HCM is similar to that of the general population (1%), although the prognosis varies substantially and each patient requires individualized risk stratification for sudden cardiac death as well as monitoring for heart failure and stroke risk factors.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An annual mortality of 10% with death from progressive systolic failure describes the prognosis of dilated cardiomyopathy, not HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While stroke is a recognized complication of HCM, the overall annual mortality is approximately 1%, not 5%, and thromboembolic stroke is not the primary cause of death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An annual mortality exceeding 15% greatly overestimates the mortality rate of HCM; the overall mortality is similar to the general population.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While overall mortality is low, it is not negligible for all patients; some high-risk patients may experience sudden cardiac death, and individual risk stratification is essential.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral lower extremity swelling over the past 6 weeks. She has a history of breast cancer treated with lumpectomy and adjuvant radiation therapy 3 years ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals bilateral crackles at the lung bases, jugular venous distension, and 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities. Chest X-ray shows mild pulmonary edema without cardiomegaly. Echocardiography reveals a left ventricular ejection fraction of 62%, bi-atrial enlargement, and elevated left ventricular filling pressures. Which of the following best describes the primary pathologic mechanism responsible for this patient's cardiac dysfunction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Myocardial fibrosis leading to decreased ventricular compliance and impaired diastolic filling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eccentric hypertrophy of the ventricular wall resulting in reduced systolic contractile function

choices.label: C
choices.text: Granulomatous infiltration of the myocardium causing conduction system abnormalities

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ischemic injury to the papillary muscles resulting in acute mitral valve regurgitation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Concentric hypertrophy of the ventricular septum causing left ventricular outflow obstruction

explanation.summary: This patient's history of thoracic radiation therapy for breast cancer, combined with heart failure symptoms, normal ejection fraction, bi-atrial enlargement, and no cardiomegaly, is consistent with restrictive cardiomyopathy due to postradiation fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Radiation therapy to the chest causes scarring and fibrosis of the ventricular myocardium, reducing compliance. The stiff ventricles cannot relax properly during diastole, leading to impaired filling, reduced end-diastolic volume, and ultimately diastolic heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Eccentric hypertrophy with reduced systolic function describes dilated cardiomyopathy. This patient has a normal ejection fraction (62%), indicating preserved systolic function, which is characteristic of RCM, not DCM.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Granulomatous infiltration is seen in sarcoidosis-related RCM. While sarcoidosis is the most common infiltrative cause, this patient's history of breast cancer and radiation therapy points to postradiation fibrosis rather than granulomatous disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ischemic papillary muscle injury causing mitral regurgitation would present with a murmur and typically follows acute myocardial infarction. The echocardiographic findings here show diastolic dysfunction with preserved ejection fraction, not valvular pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Concentric hypertrophy with outflow obstruction describes hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM). RCM has normal or slightly enlarged ventricular walls that are rigid, rather than asymmetric septal hypertrophy.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of multiple myeloma presents with worsening fatigue and exertional dyspnea over 4 months. Physical examination reveals elevated jugular venous pressure, hepatomegaly, and peripheral edema. Laboratory studies show proteinuria and elevated serum creatinine. Echocardiography demonstrates bi-atrial enlargement, normal ventricular wall thickness, diastolic dysfunction, and a preserved ejection fraction. An endomyocardial biopsy is performed. Which of the following histologic findings is most likely to be identified?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hemosiderin-laden macrophages staining positive with Prussian blue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congo red positive deposits demonstrating apple-green birefringence under polarized light

choices.label: C
choices.text: Noncaseating granulomas with multinucleated giant cells throughout the interstitium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dense collagen deposition with fibroblast proliferation replacing myocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Eosinophilic infiltration of the endocardium with overlying mural thrombi

explanation.summary: This patient with multiple myeloma, proteinuria, renal dysfunction, and features of diastolic heart failure most likely has amyloidosis causing restrictive cardiomyopathy from immunoglobulin light chain deposition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Systemic amyloidosis causes deposition of abnormal amyloid protein in the myocardium, which is identified histologically by Congo red staining that demonstrates characteristic apple-green birefringence under polarized light. Multiple myeloma is strongly associated with AL amyloidosis from immunoglobulin light chain deposition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prussian blue-positive hemosiderin deposits are seen in hemochromatosis, which causes RCM through iron overload. Hemochromatosis typically presents with cirrhosis, diabetes mellitus, and skin hyperpigmentation, not the proteinuria and myeloma history seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Noncaseating granulomas with giant cells are characteristic of sarcoidosis, the most common infiltrative cause of RCM. Sarcoidosis typically presents with pulmonary symptoms, skin findings, or eye involvement, not with multiple myeloma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dense collagen deposition with fibroblast proliferation describes fibrotic RCM, seen in conditions like postradiation fibrosis or progressive systemic sclerosis. The clinical context of multiple myeloma with proteinuria strongly favors amyloidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Eosinophilic infiltration with mural thrombi is characteristic of Loffler endocarditis, which occurs in tropical countries due to parasitic infections causing hypereosinophilia, not in the setting of multiple myeloma.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man from sub-Saharan Africa presents with progressive abdominal distension, fatigue, and exertional dyspnea. Laboratory studies reveal marked peripheral eosinophilia. Echocardiography shows bi-ventricular diastolic dysfunction with preserved ejection fraction and large intracardiac thrombi. A peripheral blood smear confirms eosinophilia. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology of this patient's cardiac disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune-mediated fibrosis from progressive systemic sclerosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transthyretin deposition from hereditary systemic amyloidosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Parasitic infection causing eosinophilic myocardial infiltration and fibrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Idiopathic collagen deposition within the ventricular endocardium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron overload from hereditary hemochromatosis causing myocyte stiffness

explanation.summary: This patient from a tropical region with marked eosinophilia, diastolic dysfunction, and large mural thrombi has Loffler endocarditis, a form of RCM caused by parasitic infections leading to eosinophilic myocardial infiltration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In tropical countries, parasitic infections (e.g., Ascaris) cause a high eosinophil count. The eosinophils infiltrate the myocardium, causing fibrosis and characteristically large mural thrombi with associated stroke risk. This condition, known as Loffler endocarditis, is a common cause of RCM in tropical zones.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Progressive systemic sclerosis is an autoimmune disease causing extensive fibrosis in skin and multiple organs. It would not explain the marked peripheral eosinophilia or large mural thrombi characteristic of this presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transthyretin amyloidosis causes protein deposition in the myocardium but does not produce eosinophilia or the large mural thrombi seen in this case. It is more common in elderly patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Idiopathic causes are the most common cause of RCM in the United States, but this patient's tropical origin, eosinophilia, and mural thrombi strongly suggest Loffler endocarditis rather than idiopathic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemochromatosis causes iron overload with hemosiderin deposition in cardiac myocytes. It classically presents with cirrhosis, diabetes, and skin hyperpigmentation, and does not cause eosinophilia.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a 10-year history of sarcoidosis presents with worsening dyspnea and chest pain. On physical examination, jugular venous pressure increases during inspiration. The physician explains to a medical student that this finding is called Kussmaul sign. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of this sign in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased right atrial pressure during inspiration causing increased venous return to the heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased intrathoracic pressure during inspiration impeding flow from the pulmonary veins

choices.label: C
choices.text: A noncompliant right atrium fails to accommodate increased venous return during inspiration, causing venous backup

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pericardial calcification restricts right ventricular expansion and reduces cardiac output during inspiration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Reduced left ventricular contractility during inspiration shifts the interventricular septum rightward

explanation.summary: Kussmaul sign is a paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration, occurring in RCM because the stiff right atrium cannot accommodate the increased venous return that normally occurs during inspiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: In RCM, the stiff right atrium does not allow the normal increase in venous return during inspiration. Inspiration normally causes a drop in intrathoracic pressure, drawing blood into the heart from the vena cava. However, in RCM, the noncompliant right atrium resists this return, and blood backs up, paradoxically increasing JVP.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes normal physiology. Under normal conditions, decreased right atrial pressure during inspiration does increase venous return, which actually causes a decrease in JVP, not the paradoxical increase seen in Kussmaul sign.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Inspiration decreases (not increases) intrathoracic pressure. This description is physiologically inaccurate and does not explain the mechanism of Kussmaul sign in restrictive cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericardial calcification restricting ventricular expansion describes constrictive pericarditis, not restrictive cardiomyopathy. While Kussmaul sign can be seen in both conditions, this patient has sarcoidosis-related RCM, not pericardial disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Septal shift due to reduced left ventricular contractility does not explain Kussmaul sign. In RCM, contractility is preserved (normal ejection fraction); the problem is impaired relaxation and filling due to myocardial stiffness.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnea. He has a history of hereditary hemochromatosis diagnosed 5 years ago but has been nonadherent with treatment. Physical examination reveals hepatomegaly, bronze skin discoloration, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Echocardiography shows normal ventricular wall thickness, preserved ejection fraction, and diastolic dysfunction. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment to address the underlying cause of this patient's cardiac disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: High-dose corticosteroid therapy to reduce myocardial inflammation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical pericardiectomy to relieve external cardiac compression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor to prevent ventricular remodeling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phlebotomy and chelation therapy to reduce systemic iron overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placement to prevent fatal arrhythmias

explanation.summary: This patient has RCM secondary to hemochromatosis, presenting with the classic triad of cirrhosis (hepatomegaly), diabetes-related findings, and bronze skin pigmentation. Phlebotomy and chelation therapy target the underlying cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: In hemochromatosis-related RCM, iron overload causes hemosiderin deposition in cardiac myocytes, leading to stiffness and diastolic dysfunction. Phlebotomy and chelation therapy remove excess iron, directly addressing the underlying cause. This is a specific targeted therapy for this secondary cause of RCM.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Corticosteroids are used for inflammatory conditions like sarcoidosis but do not address iron overload in hemochromatosis. The bronze skin, hepatomegaly, and known hemochromatosis history clearly point to iron-mediated rather than inflammatory cardiac disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pericardiectomy is a treatment for constrictive pericarditis, not restrictive cardiomyopathy. Although both conditions can present similarly, this patient has myocardial disease from iron deposition, not pericardial pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ACE inhibitors are used in systolic heart failure management but do not address the underlying iron overload causing RCM. The primary treatment must target the specific etiology of the cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ICD placement is used for patients at risk of sudden cardiac death from arrhythmias, as seen in conditions like ARVC. The priority in this hemochromatosis patient is to reduce iron overload with phlebotomy and chelation.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric cardiologist after being found to have a murmur during a routine well-child visit. Echocardiography demonstrates a thickened, fibrotic endocardium with impaired diastolic function and an associated ventricular septal defect. The ventricles are small with preserved systolic function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy with biventricular systolic dysfunction and chamber dilation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endocardial fibroelastosis with collagen deposition causing restrictive cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Endomyocardial fibrosis from parasitic infection-induced inflammation and fibrosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy with fibroadipose tissue replacement

explanation.summary: Endocardial fibroelastosis is a primary cause of RCM involving overactivation of collagen deposition in the endocardium. It is uncommon and often seen in infants and young children in conjunction with congenital heart defects.
explanation.whyCorrect: Endocardial fibroelastosis causes fibrotic stiffening of the endocardium through overactivation of collagen deposition. It is an uncommon condition often seen in infants and young children, frequently associated with congenital heart defects such as ventricular septal defect, matching this patient's presentation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy features asymmetric septal hypertrophy and potential outflow tract obstruction. This patient has a thickened fibrotic endocardium with a congenital heart defect, which is characteristic of endocardial fibroelastosis, not HCM.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dilated cardiomyopathy presents with enlarged ventricles and reduced systolic function. This patient has small ventricles with preserved systolic function and endocardial fibrosis, pointing to a restrictive rather than dilated process.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Endomyocardial fibrosis is a primary RCM caused by parasitic infections and nutritional deficiencies, predominantly in tropical regions. This pediatric presentation with a congenital heart defect is more consistent with endocardial fibroelastosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ARVC involves replacement of right ventricular myocardium with fibroadipose tissue and classically presents with arrhythmias in adolescents. The endocardial fibrosis with a congenital defect in a young child does not fit ARVC.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of progressive systemic sclerosis presents with worsening dyspnea and fatigue. She reports orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Physical examination shows an S3 gallop, pulmonary crackles, and lower extremity edema. Echocardiography reveals normal ventricular wall thickness, bi-atrial enlargement, normal ejection fraction, and elevated filling pressures. Which of the following hemodynamic changes is most directly responsible for her heart failure symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased systemic vascular resistance causing elevated afterload on the left ventricle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduced end-diastolic volume due to impaired ventricular relaxation and decreased compliance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased myocardial contractility resulting in systolic dysfunction and reduced ejection fraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral valve stenosis causing obstruction to left ventricular inflow during ventricular diastole

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased heart rate due to autonomic dysfunction leading to shortened diastolic filling time

explanation.summary: Progressive systemic sclerosis causes extensive fibrosis in multiple organs including the heart, leading to RCM. The stiff ventricles have reduced compliance, leading to reduced end-diastolic volume, decreased stroke volume, and ultimately reduced cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: In RCM, the stiff ventricular myocardium cannot expand to fill normally during diastole. This leads to reduced end-diastolic volume (EDV), which reduces stroke volume (SV) and cardiac output (CO = SV x HR). The reduced cardiac output fails to meet the body's oxygen demands, producing heart failure symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While systemic sclerosis can affect blood vessels, the cardiac dysfunction in RCM is due to myocardial stiffness causing impaired diastolic filling, not elevated afterload from increased systemic vascular resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Contractility is preserved in restrictive cardiomyopathy, as evidenced by the normal ejection fraction on echocardiography. The problem is diastolic dysfunction (impaired filling), not systolic dysfunction (impaired contraction).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral valve stenosis is a valvular disease that can cause similar symptoms but would show characteristic echocardiographic findings of a thickened, stenotic mitral valve, which is not described here. The pathology is in the myocardium.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While tachycardia can worsen diastolic filling, the primary pathology in RCM is reduced ventricular compliance, not autonomic dysfunction. The impaired relaxation is structural, not rate-related.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a history of chronic cough, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, and erythema nodosum now presents with new-onset exertional dyspnea and chest pain. ECG reveals conduction abnormalities. Echocardiography shows diastolic dysfunction with preserved ejection fraction and bi-atrial enlargement. An endomyocardial biopsy is performed. Which of the following histologic findings would most strongly support the suspected diagnosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interstitial fibrosis with dark pink myocytes separated by bands of lighter pink fibrous tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Acellular proteinaceous deposits between myofibers with apple-green birefringence on Congo red stain

choices.label: C
choices.text: Innumerable blue granules within myocytes on Prussian blue iron staining of the myocardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibroadipose tissue replacement of the myocardium with residual islands of cardiomyocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Noncaseating granulomas containing multinucleated giant cells within the myocardial interstitium

explanation.summary: This patient's chronic cough, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and now cardiac involvement with diastolic dysfunction and ECG abnormalities are consistent with cardiac sarcoidosis, the most common infiltrative cause of RCM.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis causes granulomatous inflammation, with collections of granulation tissue deposited by overactive macrophages. Cardiac sarcoidosis is the most common infiltrative cause of RCM, and biopsy shows noncaseating granulomas with multinucleated giant cells. Patients present with either cardiac symptoms or ECG abnormalities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Interstitial fibrosis is a nonspecific finding in fibrotic RCM and can be seen in multiple types of cardiomyopathy including hypertrophic and dilated cardiomyopathy, limiting its diagnostic utility. It would not confirm the specific infiltrative cause.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Congo red positive deposits with apple-green birefringence are diagnostic of amyloidosis. While amyloidosis can cause RCM, this patient's pulmonary and skin manifestations (hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum) are classic for sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prussian blue-positive iron deposits indicate hemochromatosis. This patient lacks the classic triad of cirrhosis, diabetes, and bronze skin discoloration, and her systemic features are consistent with sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibroadipose tissue replacement is the hallmark of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC), not an infiltrative cause of RCM. ARVC typically presents with ventricular arrhythmias in young patients.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with known restrictive cardiomyopathy presents with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech. CT angiography of the head confirms an acute ischemic stroke. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms best explains this complication in the context of his underlying cardiac disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture in the internal carotid artery from chronic hypertension

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale from deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Thrombus formation in a poorly contracting left ventricle with subsequent systemic embolization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vasospasm of the middle cerebral artery caused by endothelial dysfunction from amyloid deposition

choices.label: E
choices.text: Hyperviscosity syndrome from elevated immunoglobulin levels causing cerebral microvascular occlusion

explanation.summary: Up to a third of patients with RCM may present with thromboembolic phenomena like strokes, as blood clots form in the poorly moving left ventricle and travel into the systemic circulation.
